coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on metal–organic framework for selective and sensitive detection of CO3 2–
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Liping
Guo, Hao
Wu, Ning
Liu, Yinsheng
Wang, Mingyue
Liu, Bingqing
Tian, Jiaying
Wei, Xiaoqin
Yang, Wu
coredata.dc:description: Carbonate ion (CO3 2–) is an anion essential for the maintenance of life activities and is of great importance to human health. Here, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 (ECU) was prepared by introducing europium ions (Eu3+) and carbon dots (CDs) into the UiO-66-(COOH)2 parent framework under the guidance of a post-synthetic modification strategy and used for the detection of CO3 2– ion in the aqueous environment. Interestingly, when CO3 2– ions were added to the ECU suspension, the characteristic emission of carbon dots at 439 nm was significantly enhanced, while the characteristic emission of Eu3+ ions at 613 nm was reduced. Therefore, the detection of CO3 2– ions can be realized through the peak height ratio of the two emissions. The probe had a low detection limit (about 1.08 μM) and a wide linear range (0–350 μM) for the detection of carbonate. In addition, the presence of CO3 2– ions can cause a significant ratiometric luminescence response and resulted obvious red-to-blue color shift of the ECU under UV light, which will facilitate visual analysis by the naked eye.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CO3 2– detection
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Carbon dots (CDs)
Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2

Preparation of fluorescent test strips and detection of CO3 2–
Determination of CO3 2– in serum and tap water samples
Detection of CO3 2– in serum and tap water
Possible mechanism of the ratio fluorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs, (b, c) SEM images of Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 (ECU) at different magnifications, (d, e) TEM images of ECU nanoparticles at different magnifications, (f) EDS mapping of ECU.

(a) XRD patterns (b) FTIR spectra of UiO-66-(COOH)2 (U), CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 (CU) and Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 (ECU), (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and (d) the pore size distributions of U and ECU.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, UiO-66-(COOH)2 (U), (CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2) (CU) and Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 (ECU) (Excitation wavelength: 300 nm, the slit widths: 5 nm), (b) emission spectra of ECU introduced into different ion solutions, (c) comparison of fluorescence emission spectra before and after adding CO3 2– in ECU suspension (The insets are photographs before and after addition of CO3 2– under the UV-lamp), (d) CIE chromaticity diagram before and after CO3 2– addition, and (e) time-dependent fluorescence intensity of ECU suspensions in CO3 2– aqueous dispersion.
Color responses of the test strips prepared with Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 (ECU) to different concentrations of CO3 2– under UV-lamp.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 (ECU) suspensions with different CO3 2– concentrations and (b) the linear relationship of the ratio fluorescence intensity (F439 /F613) and concentration of CO3 2–.
Intramolecular energy transfer discruption in Eu complex.
Schematic illustration of synthesis process and fluorescent sensing to CO3 2– of Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of the Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 (ECU).

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on metal–organic framework for selective and sensitive detection of CO3 2–

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-functional Polymer Materials of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-functional Polymer Materials of the Ministry of Education
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Eco-functional Polymer Materials of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonate ion (CO3 2–) is an anion essential for the maintenance of life activities and is of great importance to human health. Here, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 (ECU) was prepared by introducing europium ions (Eu3+) and carbon dots (CDs) into the UiO-66-(COOH)2 parent framework under the guidance of a post-synthetic modification strategy and used for the detection of CO3 2– ion in the aqueous environment. Interestingly, when CO3 2– ions were added to the ECU suspension, the characteristic emission of carbon dots at 439 nm was significantly enhanced, while the characteristic emission of Eu3+ ions at 613 nm was reduced. Therefore, the detection of CO3 2– ions can be realized through the peak height ratio of the two emissions. The probe had a low detection limit (about 1.08 μM) and a wide linear range (0–350 μM) for the detection of carbonate. In addition, the presence of CO3 2– ions can cause a significant ratiometric luminescence response and resulted obvious red-to-blue color shift of the ECU under UV light, which will facilitate visual analysis by the naked eye.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last few years, an increased interest in the selective detection of anions has emerged due to their significant role in numerous fields [1,2]. CO3 2– is a common anion in the environment we live in every day. A lot of CO3 2– ions are produced by the carbon cycle, the breakdown of organic materials, animal respiration, etc. [3]. The conversion of carbon dioxide from the air also results in some of the CO3 2–, which can exacerbate environmental disasters like ocean acidification and climate change [4]. CO3 2– was employed in industry for a number of purposes, such as energy storage materials [5], toothpaste abrasives [6], and cosmetics [7]. Although CO3 2– has been employed in a variety of industries, the CO3 2– ion is toxic in the body in excess, which causes severe stomach upset, fainting, alkalosis, and even death [8]. More significantly, CO3 2– plays as imortant role in a variety of physiological functions related to homeostasis. For instance, CO3 2– preserves the pH of stomach juice, which helps to control the body's acid-base balance [9]. The traditional techniques were widely employed in the determination of CO3 2–, such as electrochemical method [10], Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy [11], Raman spectroscopy [12], and ICP-MS [13]. Although these techniques are exceedingly sensitive, their application is hindered due to their extraordinarily excessive cost, complicated operation, and time-consuming pretreatment. Therefore, it is essential to set up a technique with easy operation, speedy response, incredible selectivity, and high sensitivity serving as a technique to detect CO3 2–.

In latest years, ratio fluorescent probes have attracted a considerable attention due to their dominances such as fast real-time response, excellent resolution, high sensitivity, and self-calibration of some environmental elements [14,15]. Ratio fluorescence probe detection is completed by using the intensity ratio of two fluorescence peaks at distinct wavelengths for the detection of the analyte [16,17]. It can limit the built-in background interference, enhance signal-to-noise ratio, and improve detection sensitivity and accuracy [18,19]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous crystalline hybrids self-assembled from metal ions or metal clusters with organic ligands, which are remarkable candidates for the construction of efficient fluorescent sensors [20-22]. Compared with other traditional porous materials, such as activated carbon, silica, zeolite, etc., MOFs have good tunability, large specific surface area, excellent stability, easy synthesis and post functionalization [23-27]. Due to the unique pore structure and the abundant unsaturated open metal sites of MOFs, it can be assembled with other luminescent components with unique properties to form new luminescent composites [28,29]. In recent years, with the discovery of new MOFs and the deepening of sensing performance and application research, functional MOFs as fluorescence sensing materials have attracted an increasing number of attention from researchers.

Among these functional composites, luminescent guest substances play an important role in constructing highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensing platforms [30,31]. For example, Eu3+ and CDs were encapsulated into host MOFs, which effectively solved the deficiency of low luminescence activity of MOFs [32]. Eu3+ interacts with the free carboxyl groups of the organic ligands to produce an antenna effect, which in turn generates a new luminescent signal [33-36]. Because the functional groups on the CD surface are potential binding points of the target analytes, encapsulation of the guest fluorescent CDs into the host MOFs can effectively improve the detection performance [37-39]. The functionalized composites can maximize the tunable optical properties and excellent sensing and recognition capabilities of luminescent centers, as well as the specific adsorption and concentration of target analytes by MOFs [40-42].

Based on the above considerations, in this present paper, a novel dual-emission ratio fluorescent probe Eu/CDs@UiO-66-(COOH)2 (ECU) for the detection of CO3 2– ions has been synthesized by introducing CD and Eu3+ ions into the framework of UiO-66-(COOH)2 (U) by one-pot method and post-synthesis modification strategy (Scheme 1
A). At a single excitation wavelength, ECU has dual characteristic emissions of CDs and Eu3+. In addition, the significant fluorescence response generated by the carbonate ion resulted in a significant red to the blue color shift of ECU under UV-lamp, which was further verified by CIE colorimetric coordinates. The obtained CO3 2– fluorescence sensor had a linear range (0–350 μM) and detection limit (1.08 μM). Based on the advantages of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor, the probe can be used for sensitive and selective detection of CO3 2– in actual samples of serum and tap water. This research provides an effective strategy for the preparation of MOFs-based photoluminescent materials by post-synthetic modification.
The reagents and instruments used are listed in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of blue emitting carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CDs are synthesized by a modified hydrothermal method [43]. Briefly, 1.0097 g of citric acid and 0.8751 g of L-arginine were completely dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. Then, 355 μL of ethane diamine was added into the solution. The mixed solution was stirred at room temperature for 2 h, then transferred to a Teflon-lined pressure vessel of 25 mL capacity and heated at 200 °C for 5 h to form a brown product. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the product was centrifuged and then placed in a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff 1000) for 24 h for further purification. The transparent yellow aqueous solution outside the dialysis bag was collected for further use.

U and CU were synthesized by slight modified literature methods [44,45]. Firstly, 0.8512 g H4btec and 0.4508 g ZrCl4 were dissolved in 10 mL distilled water and then transferred to a round bottom flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer. Then, the mixture was heated at 100 °C for 24 h under steady stirring. The obtained product was denoted as U.

CU was prepared with a similar procedure to U except for addition of 10 mL CDs aqueous solution into the reaction system. A brown gel product will be obtained and washed several times with deionized water to remove excess free acid trapped within the pores. The obtained product was dried under a vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

ECU was prepared through a simple stirring method at room temperature [32] (Scheme 2 ). Briefly, 100 mg of CU were added into a round bottom flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer containing 10 mL of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (1 mmol, 0.446 g) aqueous solution and then the mixture was stirred at 30 °C for 24 h. The resulting ECU were washed with distilled water and ethanol for several times to guarantee that all excess Eu(NO3)3·6H2O was removed. At last, the product was vacuum dried for 6 h at 60 ℃.

The fluorescence detection of CO3 2– was implemented in deionized water at room temperature selecting 300 nm as excitation wavelength and 5 nm as the excitation and emission slit width. 5 mg ECU was dispersed in 10 mL of aqueous solution with pH = 9.86 and the obtained mixture was sonicated for about 10 min to obtain fully mixed ECU suspension. To further evaluate the selectivity of ECU, different kinds of potential interfering substances, such as metal ions, anions, etc. are respectively added to the suspension and mixed thoroughly, and then the fluorescence spectra of the mixture solution were recorded. Then, different concentrations of CO3 2– had been added and the fluorescence spectra of the mixture solutions were recorded. (Note: The experiments concerning the detection of carbonate ions were all performed at pH = 9.86.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent test strips and detection of CO3 2–
ce:section.ce:para: The 5 mg ECU material was dispersed in 10 mL distilled water and the obtained mixture was sonicated about 10 min to obtain fully mixed ECU suspension. Then, the test strips were immersed for about 24 h, and the sensitive fluorescent test strips were obtained, which could be used to detect different concentrations of CO3 2–.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CO3 2– in serum and tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application of the ECU probe, the sensing behavior of CO3 2– in serum and tap water was further studied. Serum samples were taken from healthy volunteers and processed before analysis. At first, the serum samples were treated to take away the protein. 1.5 mL acetonitrile was added into 1 mL serum sample, and the resulted mixture was ultrasonically treated for 5 min. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 30 min, the analytical solution of serum was obtained [46]. The tap water sample was filtered with a 0.22 μm injection filter. Then different concentrations (10, 20, 30 μM) of CO3 2– ion solutions were added to the serum and tap water sample solutions, and the fluorescence intensities were recorded at 439 nm and 613 nm, respectively.

The target material ECU was synthesized by modifying CDs and Eu3+ into the framework of U. Fig. 1
a was a TEM image of CDs with an average particle size below 5 nm and aggregation of the synthesized CDs can be observed. Fig. 1 b and Fig. 1 c were SEM images of ECU at different amplifications. It can be clearly observed that ECU had an uniform and rough microsphere morphology after modification. Fig. 1 d and e were TEM images of ECU at different magnifications. CDs doping in MOF can be observed, which further indicated the successful preparation of ECU. In addition, the element mapping showed that C, Zr, O, Eu and N elements all existed in the particles in Fig. 1 f.

The crystal structures of CU and ECU were illustrated by XRD in Fig. 2
a. The XRD pattern of U showed three characteristic peaks at 2θ = 7.68°, 8.64° and 25.72°, which were attributed to (111), (200) and (600) crystal plane diffractions respectively. The XRD patterns of the synthesized U, CU and ECU coincided with the simulated result of U, which completely proved that the synthesis of U was successful, and the addition of CD and Eu3+ had no effect on the U framework structure.

Infrared spectra were employed to study the structure and formation of the synthesized materials. Fig. S1 showed the FT-IR spectrum of CDs. Appearance of the broad absorption band around 3400 cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibration of the N–H and O–H bonds, indicated that there were hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs. The peak at 2964 cm−1 resulted from C-H stretching vibration. The strong peak at 1658 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching vibration. Besides, the peak at 1555 cm−1 was ascribed to the N–H bending vibration, and the peak at 1400 cm−1 belonged to a typical C-N stretching vibration [43]. These results clearly demonstrated the successful synthesis of CDs. In IR spectra of U, CU and ECU as shown in Fig. 2 b appeared two absorption bands at ∼1585 and ∼1511 cm−1, respectively, which were attributed to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the coordinated carboxyl groups of 1,4-position of H4btec ligand with Zr ion in the U framework [45]. At the same time, all the characteristic peaks of CDs also existed in CU and ECU, indicating that CDs and U coexisted simultaneously in CU. In addition, the carboxyl group of 1, 4-positions of H4btec ligand with Zr constituted the basic framework of U. Besides above characteristic absorption bands, the strong –OH absorption band at ∼3400 cm−1 and CO stretching vibration vibration band at 1716 cm−1 exhibited that existence of free carboxyl groups in U and CU. It was because that the two carboxyl groups at 2, 5-positions of H4btec ligand in U and CU did not coordinate with Zr(IV), which allowed the MOF to be further functionalized with Eu3+ subsequently [47]. The pore properties of the synthesized materials were studied with N2 adsorption–desorption method. Fig. 2 c showed N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of U and ECU, from which BET specific surface areas of U and ECU were calculated as 506.46 m2/g and 361.90 m2/g, respectively. Obviously, CDs and Eu3+ modification led to a decreased specific surface area. The pore size distributions of U and ECU were analyzed from the desorption isotherms using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method and showed in the Fig. 2 d. It could be seen that the pore size of ECU decreased compared with the unmodified U. It was speculated that the pores were blocked by CDs and Eu3+, which reduced the specific surface area and pore size of the composite. The thermal stability of the final material was evaluated with thermal gravimetry. As showed in Fig S2 , thermal stability of the material framework almost did not change with addition of Eu3+ and CDs. The first weight loss step at 120 ℃ was attributed to the loss of small quantities of solvent molecules such as water and ethanol adsorbed in the ECU surface. The second step takes place in the range of 120 ℃–300 ℃ due to the decomposition of CDs and volatilization of the adsorbates in the cavity. The third step weight loss at ∼500 ℃ might result from the decomposition of organic ligands and the skeleton collapse in the framework. These results demonstrated that the prepared ECU had acceptable thermal stability.

The luminescence spectra of aqueous solutions of CDs, U, CU and ECU under the same conditions were shown in Fig. 3
a. At 300 nm excitation, bare CDs had a strong emission peak at 439 nm, while the independent U had a wide weak emission band, which resulted from the π*→π transition of the ligand [47]. Interestingly, when CDs are encapsulated in U, the resulted CU also revealed a strong fluorescence emission peak at 429 nm. Additionally, when Eu3+ ions were encapsulated into the pores of CU via post-synthesis modification (PSM), the resulted ECU exhibited two strong characteristic emissions under excitation at 300 nm. Besides the strong emission at 439 nm the material also showed some sharp emission peaks at 582 nm, 590 nm, 613 nm, 650 nm, and 700 nm attributed to characteristic emissions of 5D0 → 7F0, 5D0 → 7F1, 5D0 → 7F2, 5D0 → 7F3, 5D0 → 7F4 of Eu3+, respectively.

The luminescence stability of the material under different extreme conditions was further investigated to assess the usefulness of the material as an aqueous medium fluorescent probe. The composite ECU was uniformly dispersed in aqueous solutions with different pH values and then its fluorescence spectra were recorded in Fig. S3 . It could be seen that pH (1–10) had almost no effect on the ratio fluorescence intensity (F439/F613). As a result, the material had a good stability over a wide pH range and could be used for testing in acidic to alkaline environments. Meanwhile, the influence of NaCl concentrations was further studied in Fig. S4 . In the range of 0–1 M, the fluorescence intensity of ECU remained unchanged, indicating that it had a good salt tolerance. Therefore, the sensor had a high feasibility in practical sample analysis.

CO3 2– plays an important role in regulating the acid-base balance and maintaining the homeostasis of the human body and can maintain the relatively stable pH of the human gastric juice. Therefore, it is very necessary to develop a specific luminescence sensor based on nanomaterials to achieve the detection of CO3 2–. Firstly, in order to demonstrate the selectivity of ECU to CO3 2–, 500 μL (10-2 M) of common anion and cation aqueous solution (CO3 2–, HCO3 –, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, F-, Cl-, Br-, I- and Ca2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Ag+, K+, Na+) was added to 5 mL of solution containing 500 μL of ECU suspension (1 mg.mL −1), respectively. After the mixture was sonicated for about 10 min, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in Fig. 3 b. It was found that only CO3 2– led to a significant enhancement of the fluorescence intensity at 439 nm and a slight decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 613 nm. Fig. 3 c further demonstrated this result by a comparison of the fluorescence emission spectra of the composites before and after adding CO3 2– solution. The insets clearly also showed a deepening of the blue fluorescence of ECU solution in the presence of CO3 2– under the UV-lamp. In addition, Fig. 3 d, the CIE chromaticity diagram of the system before (X1 = 0.3286, Y1 = 0.251) and after (X2 = 0.1772, Y2 = 0.1238) addition of CO3 2– also showed that the coordinates moved to the blue region after the addition of CO3 2–. Meanwhile, dependence of the ratio fluorescence intensity I439/I613 on the reaction time of CO3 2– ions with ECU was also explored and the results were shown in Fig. 3 e. It was observed showed that the ratio intensity reached stability at 210 s, so the luminescence response was rapid enough toward CO3 2–. Besides, luminescence response of the prepared test strips to different concentrations of CO3 2– under UV-lamp was also observed and the results were shown in Fig. 4 . It was seen that the color of the test strips gradually changed from light red to blue and blue fluorescence was enhanced with the increase of CO3 2– concentration, which was consistent with the conclusion of the above analysis. Therefore, it also is possible to use the fabricated sensor for an on-site detection of CO3 2–, which makes the detection process more convenient and fast.

The anti-interference ability of ECU for detection of CO3 2– was further explored in Fig. S5 . By a comparison, it was found that without adding CO3 2–, the ratio emission intensity of the system was slightly reduced by other ions. However, it was worth noting that the fluorescence intensity at 439 nm was dramatically enhanced when CO3 2– and other ion coexisted in the system, so that the F439/F613 value was also significantly enhanced. Since CO3 2– and HCO3 – can be converted to each other, the effect of HCO3 – on CO3 2– detection was also studied in Fig. S6 . When CO3 2– and HCO3 – were added simultaneously to ECU suspension, the fluorescence of CDs center was enhanced and the Eu center luminescence was inhibited. In contrast, after adding only HCO3 – to ECU, the fluorescence spectra of CDs did not change significantly. So HCO3 – had no effect on the detection of CO3 2– and this method could selectively detect CO3 2– by ratiometric fluorescence.

Subsequently, the sensitivity of ECU for CO3 2– determination was further assessed. In Fig. 5
a, the fluorescence intensity of ECU at 439 nm gradually increased with the increase of CO3 2– concentration from 0 to 500 μM, while the fluorescence intensity at 613 nm slightly decreased. A good linear relationship was observed between F439/F613 and CO3 2– concentration in the range of 0–350 μM (R2 = 0.9929) as showed in Fig. 5 b and the detection limit (LOD) was estimated to be 1.08 μM based on LOD = 3Sb/K. Of course, total concentration of CO3 2– is also easy to know with the equation

. Dissociation constants of carbonate ion, Ka1 and Ka2 are 4.2 × 10-7 and Ka2 = 3.5 × 10−11, respectively. Table S1 revealed a comparison of analytical performances of the prepared ratiometric fluorescence sensor with the reported results. It was found that the proposed method had a wider linear range and lower detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CO3 2– in serum and tap water
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the practical application value of this sensor, contents of CO3 2– in serum and tap water were detected by the standard addition method. Different concentrations of CO3 2– were spiked into the actual samples and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The experimental results were shown in Table S2 . The recoveries of CO3 2– in serum and tap water samples were 98.91–103.6 % and 98.53–102.4 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were lower than 2.66 %. These results indicated that the ratio fluorescence probe was accurate and feasible for CO3 2– detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the ratio fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The possible sensing mechanism was studied. As shown in Fig. S7 , ECU microspheres became a slice structure after the addition of CO3 2– ions, which indicated that the presence of CO3 2– significantly changed the morphology of the composites. In addition, the structure change of the composite CO3 2– caused was also investigated by XRD. Fig. S8 showed that the XRD pattern of ECU after being immersed in CO3 2– solution was obviously different from that before immersion, indicating that the framework of the ECU had been severely damaged by CO3 2–. It is evident that the addition of CO3 2– led to the structural destruction and morphological changes of ECU, which may make Eu3+ in ECU release, thereby weakening the antenna effect between Eu3+ ions and ligands in Fig. 6 . So its fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced at 613 nm. Furthermore, the destruction of the structure resulted in the release of CDs encapsulated in the MOFs pores, which in turn resulted in a significant enhancement of the characteristic fluorescence peak of CDs at 439 nm.

In short, a dual emission fluorescence probe for rapid detection of CO3 2– based on ECU composite was successfully constructed by encapsulating CDs and Eu3+ into pores of UiO-66-(COOH)2. The incorporation of CDs and Eu3+ led to strong fluorescence emissions of the resulted ECU composite at 439 nm and 613 nm simultaneously. Based on different influences of CO3 2– on the intensities of these two fluorescence emissions the prepared ECU was employed as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective determination of CO3 2–. This work provides a new strategy for the development of MOF-based fluorescent sensors for convenient detection of CO3 2– in serum and tap water.

Liping Peng: Writing – review & editing. Hao Guo: Funding acquisition. Ning Wu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yinsheng Liu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mingyue Wang: Formal analysis, Software. Bingqing Liu: Project administration, Investigation, Resources. Jiaying Tian: Project administration, Investigation, Resources. Xiaoqin Wei: Investigation, Resources. Wu Yang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21665024), Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province (20JR10RA083), Project of Gansu Excellent Doctoral (22JR5RA140), Northwest Normal University Young Teachers Research Capacity Promotion Plan (NWNU-LKQN-18-23).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122844.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A luminescent lanthanide complex- based anion sensor with electron-donating methoxy groups for monitoring multiple anions in environmental and biological processes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zheng, C. Tan, G.P. Drummen, Q. Wang, A luminescent lanthanide complex- based anion sensor with electron-donating methoxy groups for monitoring multiple anions in environmental and biological processes, Spectrochim. Acta A. 96 (2012) 387-394.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress on reaction-based BODIPY probes for anion detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gallic acid capped Tb3+-doped CaF2 nanocrystals: an efficient optical probe for the detection of carbonate and bicarbonate ions
